sentences.text: So where we were now?
What did I say?
No I go to Bregenz.
I leave Yan and the [NON-ENGLISH] in [NON-ENGLISH] and go to Bregenz for a job.
I went to Bregenz, to the Polish Red Cross, looking for a job.
And was offered to take children, orphans, mainly orphans, to Italy, to a camp for displaced persons.
I was very happy about this job, because I was also told that there is a Polish school where Yan could go, a high school.
And I was very keen of Yan having some education.
I still look for my husband, but after a week or two, the transport went off.
I don't know how many days we travelled, but it was not too bad.
It was summer.
And we arrived in Barletta, where I left the children and Mr. and Mrs.
[NON-ENGLISH].
And I went on with Yan to Trani.
He went to his boarding school.
And I went to the office in the Red Cross.
The next day, I took Yan to the hospital, to the Polish hospital, because he was very short-sighted.
And during the war, he couldn't wear glasses.
Because it made him looking more Jewish.
I met there a doctor, I knew from Lvov, a specialist, who looked at me like coming from the other world.
Asked, how did you survive?
And where is your husband?
And I say, well, never, how I survived.
I don't know where my husband is.
I looked for him everywhere.
And I knew he was in England.
This doctor knew that he was an X-ray specialist.
So he said, well, we have here an X-ray specialist.
Dr.
Corbin Koronowski, Corbin Koronowski And he came just back from England.
Perhaps he knows something about your husband.
I take you there, before he even looked at Yan's eyes.
That was more important now.
We came to Dr.
Corbin Koronowski.
I was introduced as Mrs.
Schutz.
He looked at me and said, are you a relative of Marx Schutz?
Well, I said, yes.
I'm his wife.
And that is his son.
Do you know anything about him?
He said, I saw Marx a week ago.
Now, well you can imagine how I felt.
Yan was, I think, dancing around on one leg, so glad.
And Dr.
Koronowski said, I will send at once a telegram.
Because civilians were not allowed to use any post.
It was only for the services.
Well, Dr.
Rice talked to me.
We had a good meal.
Well, anyway, I wasn't hungry any more in Italy, or even in [NON-ENGLISH] I wasn't hungry.
But we had a meal.
We talked about old times.
His wife was also a doctor.
They live now in London.
And he got glasses, Yan.
And next day I had a telegram from my husband.
Well, I have this telegram.
Still I have a lot of his telegrams.
And we started to correspond only through the forces, the civilians.
He tried at once to get the visa for me.
No visa for no-- no visa for aliens.
Also he had a job, he was in the forces, he didn't ask for anything.
He just wanted his-- he could afford to have his wife there.
But no-- no luck.
In the meantime, I didn't tell you that George, my mother-in-law, Milek and Lucia, survived the Holocaust in Poland.
And they're living in Lodz, which was called, Litzmannstadt during the war.
There was a lot of Germans before the war.
So they started at once, no.
Yes, they started at once looking for me, and couldn't find me.
They knew my false name.
I had disappeared.
But they got a letter from a priest who was in Auschwitz, that Eva Cernetska was in the camp and killed herself by touching the-- the wires.
But she never said that she has a son.
Which was no-- nothing special, because nobody said that they had a family.
So Lucia was sure that I'm dead.
And she sent this letter to my husband, who she found in the meantime, and he found her, and so on.
I don't know how they did it.
But he knew that his wife is dead, and no sign of his son, they thought it would be a good idea to bring George to England to give him a good education.
Because Lucia always thought that England, she was a bit of a snob.
England is the best for education.
I don't agree with her.
But anyway, so my husband did everything to get the papers for George.
And there was a rabbi Shoenfeld.
As you must have heard of him, who brought Jewish orphans over.
But that comes later.
But I was, in the meantime, in Italy.
I was quite happy.
I had a good job.
I was very much liked.
The job was not too-- too heavy, too difficult, rather.
Yan went to school.
I knew my husband is alive.
I just wasn't very patient.
My husband came for two or three weeks, went back.
And then suddenly, I got a visa.
My husband sent me the tickets, and some postal orders.
I didn't know what a postal order is.
And I went via Paris.
I found a friend, a lady, who was called, [NON-ENGLISH], who later came to England, also.
She had friends in Paris.
She gave me their address.
Today I have no idea where I was, what these people were.
I just wanted to get to England.
I sent a telegram from Paris, to my husband, arriving in-- in Dover, I think.
This and this, with this and this ship.
Well, I boarded to ship, we went to Dover-- my husband wasn't there.
I had to take the train who was connected, so to say, with this ship.
And went to Victoria.
No husband of mine there.
I was really desperate.
I didn't know what to do.
I didn't know anybody in London.
But then I found a card, which I got from a friend of my husband, to-- in Italy.
His nephew was in Italy.
And I had his address, 66 Boiler Road.
That's in Ealing.
I had his address.
And I showed it to a taxi.
There were queues-- taxi.
I don't know, where you were born, when this time was?
So anyway, you didn't see the taxis there.
It was night.
And no taxi driver wanted to take me to this address from Victoria to Ealing.
This woman standing-- I was in uniform.
And Yan was in uniform.
I was a leftenant.
So they didn't want me.
But a policeman took-- policeman kept [INAUDIBLE], took pity on me, and told one of these taxis, you have to take this woman there.
And asked how much will it cost?
And he said, a pound.
Well, he could have said 10 pounds, I didn't care what it was.
I didn't know what 10 pounds is, and what 1 pound is.
So he took me there.
It was a long journey.
It was night.
I was frightened.
I was sure he will kill me and take those 10 pounds off me or 1 pound, and my sack.
I had this service-- I had no-- I had a sack, [INAUDIBLE].
And he took me to Boiler Road 66.
It was midnight, or even early morning.
Rung the bell, and Dr.
Felix was the name of this-- a lawyer.
Opened the door, and saw me standing there, and didn't know who that is.
So well, I say him, Marx's wife.
Oh!
Marx doesn't know you're coming.
Well, I sent him a telegram.
I got a bath, I got something to eat.
We went to bed.
He phoned my-- phoned, I suppose.
My husband couldn't come for one or two days, because he was-- he had patients in the hospital.
He couldn't leave.
There was only one X-ray specialist.
If there would have been another, he could come.
So he came after two days.
And he had a room reserved with a friend.
He knew his wife will come, near Lancaster Gate.
This house doesn't exist.
It's only now where the big hotel is.
So we stayed a few days in-- in London.
Yan got new glasses, also.
And went underground to escalator, up and down.
He never, I never saw an escalator.
But for him it was fun.
I don't think we went to the [INAUDIBLE].
I even don't know where it was, but my husband showed me.
We could go into-- to a restaurant, I think, you could get food only 2 pound-- 2 shillings and 6 pence, or I don't know what amount it was.
Very little food.
And then we went to the hospital, which was fine.
Took [INAUDIBLE] from Euston to [INAUDIBLE] and from there, the ambulance was waiting.
They knew my husband is coming with his family.
And there I was-- we lived in a-- my husband had a big barrack, a [NON-ENGLISH].
Nissen hut, yes.
A nissen hut.
And it was all right there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My husband was very friendly with an English officer, who was in charge there.
Not as a doctor, but it was the English in charge.
And he arranged for both boys.
Because my husband knew that George is coming.
He couldn't say now, no.
Therefore, both boys to be accepted to [INAUDIBLE] grammar school in Warminster-- Warminster.
And that's where Yan went after a few days in hospital, staying with us.
But then George arrived after a bit of trouble in [INAUDIBLE].
We couldn't find him.
But he arrived at the hospital.
And he went to the school.
They were and not very happy in the beginning.
They didn't speak English.
But very soon, George was a prefect.
Very soon they were-- they were very good pupils.
They learned English quick.
They came for the holidays, to [INAUDIBLE] park, where we had a room for them.
They stayed-- it was on a farm, a room.
But they ate at the officers' canteen.
And life went on, till my husband found he had a growth behind the ear.
It was cancer.
He had one operation.
But it came back.
I went with him to London, where I had a friend from childhood, which I found in the meantime, Mimi-- I still-- she's still my friend.
She's still alive.
She married and had a baby.
I stayed with her.
My husband was at the Royal Marsden Hospital, which was called, General Cancer Hospital, which I found was very cruel.
He had one operation, and went back to work.
And had a second operation, and went back to work.
And then in '49, it was already terminal.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the beginning of '50, in February, 1950, he died in the hospital.
The boys were, in the meantime, finished in Warminster, and went to Shrewsbury to college.
The both boys came before, just the day when Marx died, stayed for the funeral, went back to college.
And I had to make, there was the funeral, he's buried in Rexam.
And they-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had to think, now what to do.
So soon my cousins, Sir Louis, name, yeah?
Rather, through his wife, I heard about-- I got a letter from a lady who was called, Mrs.
Grenfell, who needed a housekeeper.
I was looking for a job where I could keep Yan with me.
As in a hotel in a pension, in a home, never mind.
And I got-- she sent me 3 pounds for the journey.
And I went and had an interview.
And I was accepted at once.
She heard that I can cook, and I can cook Viennese cook, and I can make Wiener-schnitzel And she was very happy.
They had the most beautiful flat, on the corner of Pond Street and Cadogan Place.
They had [INAUDIBLE].
And they had, it you name it.
It was there.
Her daughter-in-law was Joyce Grenfell.
Her daughter married the Earl of Waldegrave.
And I was, for a few days, in charge of their children, the two boys, little, Billy, Wilhelm Waldegrave, who is now the minister in the conservative party.
I helped to put him on the high chair, and fed him.
They liked both boys.
He was the honorable, and his older brother was Viscount.
And then there were three sisters, much older, they were Lady Caroline, and Lady Ann, and Lady something.
For me that was all Chinese.
I couldn't understand all that.
When visitors came, they brought portions of tea with them, because tea was rationed.
Mrs.
Grenfell had more tea than they have in China.
But it was quite-- they were very good to me, the Grenfells, very good.
They loved both boys.
They wanted them to eat at their table, which I never allowed.
When they came-- Yan left, stayed with me.
I had a flat in the basement, two rooms and a bathroom.
And naturally, enough food for Yan, also.
He went-- George was already accepted at Bartholomew, but he had to wait a year.
Because there was no place.
Yes.
That was the best one, and a Pole, there be accepted was the best you can-- everybody who knows George now, knows, oh, you're a Bart's man.
And Yan was studying still at college, but was accepted then, at the Imperial College.
So--
Engineering, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] aeronautical.
But anyway, I stayed with the Grenfells for a year and a half.
They were always very nice to me.
I went to the Waldegrave Estate for summer, because the Waldegraves went for the summer to Scotland.
And I stayed there with only the two of them, the old-- well, Colonel, and Mrs.
Grenfell, and my little dog- I had a dog with me-- and a friend of mine.
Because Mrs.
Grenfell thought I may be very lonely there, so bring a friend with you, Eva.
And that was my stay with the Grenfells.
Then they-- they let their flat in Pond Street, or Cadogan Place, to diplomats, and went to Betteshanger, which is in-- near Deal.
And I went with them.
I didn't want to stay there.
It was-- it was very lonely.
And I didn't like it.
I didn't like to be there.
London was something different.
When I was free, I went out.
I saw things.
And I wanted to do something with my life.
A friend of mine-- an acquaintance of mine-- wanted to go to Israel, and let the house, her house to somebody who took care of the house, and for a very low rent.
So I grabbed this situation.
I gave notice to Mrs.
Grenfell, who was very unhappy to lose me.
And I had a room for Yan, a room for George, and a lodger was there, whose rent paid nearly the rent which I paid Mrs.
Kaufman.
And I got in touch with Mrs.
Sudorf, who was the dressmaker, and we started dressmaking.
Which didn't pay.
After a year, I gave that up, and went as a cutter to a firm, Madame Felicia, off Regent Street, a firm, which I was with later 15 years.
But I couldn't stay at Mrs.
Kaufman longer than a year, because she came back.
I stayed for a little while.
And I looked for a flat where I could stay with the boys, and go to work.
And really, I was lucky, because I found one.
I had just 300 pounds.
That was what my husband got when he was demobbed.
And he left that.
That was all he left.
And I had his money.
So I gave for this flat 200 pounds.
It was in-- well, it was not legal, but these people bought a house, or whatever, and I got a flat for a very small rent.
I think it was 9 pounds a month, in Elgin Crescent, that is near Portobello Road.
It was a Maisonette.
Each of the boys had a room.
There was a very big kitchen, which was also the dining room.
It was-- and I had my room, and a bathroom.
The landlady was very old.
I suppose she was much younger than I am now.
And life went on.
I worked.
The boys studied, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] worked in their free time.
That means there was no free time.
Yan was a conductor, was a postman, was a-- worked at Wally's Ice Cream, Eldorado Ice Cream.
They had to earn some money, because I earned 7 pounds 10 a week, which was not enough for three people.
But they gave me 10 pounds, and 10 pounds was-- they got a grant from a Polish organization, for 20 pounds a month.
So I got 10 pounds, and they had 10 pounds for their lunches, and whatever they wanted.
So there goes my life in Elgin Crescent.
Then George was already a doctor.
He lived in a hospital, as house doctor.
And Yan finished the Imperial College, worked in St.
Albans at a firm which made airplanes, which he didn't like.
And one day he told me he is going to immigrate.
That was in '56 or '55, to immigrate to Canada.
Out of the blue, I had no idea.
He had everything arranged.
He had the medical.
He had a job in Toronto-- in Montreal.
And he's going.
So what could I do?
I had a life insurance taken out, because when my husband died, I thought, if something happens to me, the boys can't study.
I took one out for, I think I paid 50 pounds a year.
So I took 100 pounds loan on that, and gave it to Yan, so he will have something to get on with.
The journey was paid by the government.
We had to pay only 10 pounds.
And he had to pay it back in two years.
Well, he got the job.
He got a better job in Montreal.
He didn't go to Toronto.
He was supposed to go to Toronto.
He stopped in Montreal.
After two years, he paid off the journey.
He sent me an invitation.
Not an invitation, a ticket.
And I went to visit him.
But in the meantime, I left this flat, because the boys were not there anymore.
I had a lodger.
I didn't like it-- colleague of Yan or of George, I can't remember.
And I had the opportunity to get a flat in Chiswick, in Duke's Avenue.
A friend of mine bought it.
He was a lawyer, and legal-- my legal advisor.
He bought it, and he offered me a flat on the ground floor.
So I could also help him a bit.
He was really an elderly gentleman, to help with the running of the house.
So I-- now I sold my flat.
I got, I don't know what for it, not so much.
But I got enough to buy a little bit of furniture for this flat, which was a converted house.
I got the ground floor flat, two rooms and the kitchen, the bathroom.
When did you come here?
To this flat?
11 years ago.
Later.
So I was on Duke's Avenue.
The garden was a jungle, but with the help of other people, or some people, had later, a wonderful garden.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] George was an established doctor.
He worked in Hammersmith Hospital in the Brompton Hospital, everywhere.
And then he settled down in the center, Middlesex Hospital.
And now he is already retired.
And he is an eminent doctor, known all over the world.
Yan married in 1960, a Swiss girl, who was a teacher in Montreal.
No.
Yan is an atheist.
So is Francoise.
Yes.
And she is a lovely daughter-in-law.
I couldn't have a better one.
Yan built up-- he was first employed, and Francoise worked as a teacher.
So they saved.
Yan had his own business.
He bought a business.
Then he sold the business.
Well, as businesses go, later he worked in metal detectors, and did very well.
After nine year of marriage, they adopted a child-- they couldn't have children.
So they adopted a child.
It's Barbara.
This year she was married.
I went for her marriage.
In the meantime, I went nearly every year to Canada for my holidays, usually at Christmas.
And-- but I was still in Dukes Avenue.
Well, 11 years ago, it was too much for me to look after the tenants, and the house.
And Yan and Francoise wanted me to have a quieter life.
I sold this flat, advertised.
I had friends here in Watchfield Court, because I played bridge.
In the meantime, I learned to play bridge.
And I knew Watchfield Court.
I thought it's marvelous to be here.
And when I heard that there is a flat next to Marcel, which was a great bridge player, and already a friend of mine, I ask her, what is this flat like?
She said, it very nice.
And you know the person who lives here?
Yes, I knew her.
And I phoned Yan, because Yan was to buy the flat.
I didn't have the money.
So I phoned Yan.
And he said, if you like it, I'll send the money straight away.
Which is rent and deposit, and in three weeks the flat was mine.
They moved out.
They wanted cash.
That's why they gave it a bit.
So they moved out.
The flat was in good condition.
But it was not my-- there was pink and green, the walls, and so on, and so on.
I wanted it white.
I had it all-- there was no repairs, too, only in the toilet.
And here there was a-- a-- a fireplace.
I didn't want a fireplace.
So that was done.
And I moved in on the 1st of October, '85.
So I'm now 11 years and something here.
Well, yes.
After I retired, I retired in '69.
I worked only part time.
Suddenly I had, I was really alone.
I had a few acquaintances.
But I thought I have to have somebody to talk to.
And I looked for a synagogue.
I was told in Brook Green, here in Hammersmith, there was a synagogue.
I went there.
When I said I want to be cremated, he swore his hand on, no!
They are-- what is it?
Orthodox.
Orthodox one, so I can't be cremated.
So I said, well, where can I be cremated?
[LAUGHS] Which synagogue will cremate me?
I had no idea about all of those things.
So I was told the liberal synagogue in Ealing.
I phoned them, or I wrote a letter.
And I was treated with open arms.
And I had really, my friends are from there now.
I am not in touch with them now, so much, because first of all, a few died.
My best friend from the synagogue died.
But her husband still-- I'm in touch with him.
And a few other.
But I go seldom to the synagogue.
It's too-- it's too difficult for me.
I have no transport there.
I don't drive.
I drive, because when I was, I think 70, I decided to take driving lessons.
And George said, you never will get a permission.
And I passed the test.
So I could drive, but I never wanted a car.
Yan wanted to buy me a car.
I buy you a little car, and you will drive.
I didn't want the responsibility of a car, and all that.
I take a mini-cab.
